original_abstract: Developing high-energy-density cathode materials that maintain structural integrity during the rapid intercalation and deintercalation of sodium ions remains a significant challenge for the advancement of sodium-ion batteries. In this study, we report the design of a P2-layered oxide cathode, Na₀.₇Li₀.₀₃[Mg₀.₁₅Li₀.₀₇Mn₀.₇₅]O₂, featuring a unique lithium dual-site substitution where Li occupies both transition-metal (TM) and alkali-metal (AM) sites. Using a combination of density functional theory calculations and advanced experimental characterization, we demonstrate that Li ions in the TM sites create Na−O−Li electronic configurations that activate and enhance anionic oxygen redox, significantly boosting specific capacity. Simultaneously, Li ions in the AM sites act as rigid LiO₆ prismatic pillars that stabilize the layered framework by preventing deleterious phase transitions typically observed at high and low voltages. Consequently, this material delivers a high specific capacity of 266 mAh g⁻¹ while maintaining a nearly zero-strain characteristic (only 1.2% volume variation) across a wide 1.5–4.6 V potential range. These results illustrate that a dual-site substitution strategy can effectively resolve the traditional trade-off between capacity and structural stability in advanced rechargeable battery cathodes.
humanized_abstract: Achieving high-energy-density cathode materials with robust structural integrity against the fast intercalation and deintercalation of sodium ions remains a major hurdle for sodium-ion batteries (SIBs). In this work, we explore a new strategy to develop a P2-layered oxide cathode material with the formula of Na₀.₇Li₀.₀₃[Mg₀.₁₅Li₀.₀₇Mn₀.₇₅]O₂. Through combining density functional theory (DFT) simulations and experiments, the lithium dual-site substitution with Li at the transition-metal (TM) and alkali-metal (AM) sites not only introduces the Na−O−Li electronic configurations benefited to the activation of anionic oxygen redox, leading to the increase in specific capacity, but also enhances the structural integrity of the material by using Li ions in AM sites of AM to act as the rigid LiO₆ prismatic pillars, which prohibit undesirable phase transitions in both high and low charge states. Therefore, the Na₀.₇Li₀.₀₃[Mg₀.₁₅Li₀.₀₇Mn₀.₇₅]O₂ exhibits a specific capacity of 266 mAh g⁻¹ with only a slight volume expansion of only 1.2% within a wide operating voltage window ranging from 1.5 to 4.6 V. This study clearly shows that the achieved battery performance demonstrates that the dual-site substitution could successfully eliminate the trade-off between high capacity and structural integrity.
